prs.title: feat: add JSON Schema Test Suite compliance badges
prs.body:
Adds per-draft test suite badges to the README as discussed in #76 .



Each badge shows passing tests against the official JSON-Schema-Test-Suite for draft-04, draft-06, draft-07, draft-2019-09 and draft-2020-12.

**How it works:**
There are two new files — `.github/workflows/badges.yaml` which runs after CI and executes each draft's test suite, and `.github/scripts/parse-test-results.js` which parses the output and figures out the pass count and badge color. Results get pushed to a GitHub Gist via `schneegans/dynamic-badges-action` and shields.io reads from there.

I've left the Gist config as `GIST_SECRET` and `BADGE_GIST_ID` secrets so you can point it to your own Gist after merging — the README URLs would just need updating to match. Happy to do that once you have the Gist set up, or I can keep mine in temporarily if that's easier.

Would love to hear your thoughts on this!

prs.title: support custom vocabularies via esm.sh with user trust prompt
prs.body:
For the prompt I used window/showMessageRequest server sends it, VSCode renders the Allow/Deny dialog, no client changes needed for that part. For persisting the response I used globalState via two custom notifications between the server and extension since the server can't access globalState directly. On startup the extension pushes already-trusted identifiers to the server so the user doesn't get asked again.

One annoying thing Node.js doesn't allow import() over https: and esm.sh returns a redirect file instead of actual code. So I fetch the redirect, pull out the real path, fetch that, and load it as a data: URL.
New files are language-server/src/services/vocabulary-loader.js and .vscode/launch.json (no debug config existed in the repo). vscode/src/extension.ts handles the globalState side.

Would love a review on this pretty sure the overall approach is right but there might be edge cases I've missed, especially around the esm.sh fetching logic and how the notifications are structured between server and extension.

prs.title: feat: add strictRefs option to error on unknown $ref target (closes #91)
prs.body:
Adds a strictRefs option to compileSchema that surfaces unresolvable $ref targets as a JsonError instead of silently passing.
Usage:
```
const schema = compileSchema(mySchema, { strictRefs: true });
const { valid, errors } = schema.validate(data);
// errors[0].code === "unknown-ref-target-error" if $ref target not foun

```


Changes:
src/compileSchema.ts — added strictRefs to CompileOptions and context
src/SchemaNode.ts — added strictRefs?: boolean to Context type
src/keywords/$ref.ts — validateRef and reduceRef return error when strictRefs: true
src/errors/errors.ts — added unknown-ref-target-error message
src/tests/issues/issue82.test.ts — added two tests

prs.title: fixesIssue #2579
prs.body:
**What issue does this pull request resolve?**

Fixes #2579.

When `oneOf` is combined with `unevaluatedProperties: false`, Ajv previously merged evaluation results only from the subschema that validated.  
According to Draft 2020-12 §6.5.3.2.2, instance locations processed by an applicator should be considered evaluated regardless of whether that subschema ultimately succeeds.  
Because of this, properties handled in failing branches could later be rejected as unevaluated.



**What changes did you make?**

The `oneOf` validity rule (exactly one subschema must pass) is unchanged.

This PR updates evaluation propagation so that information from processed subschemas is not lost.  
For failing branches, only statically known evaluated properties/items are merged.  
Dynamic or universal evaluations (e.g. represented by `Name` or `true`) remain gated on `schValid`, preserving Ajv’s existing scoping expectations and preventing leakage from branches that do not contribute to the result.

I also added a regression test covering the reported scenario.



**Is there anything that requires more attention while reviewing?**

Evaluation propagation around composite keywords is subtle, especially with dynamic references.

I verified locally that:
- the reported false negative is resolved,
- the exactly-one constraint is unaffected,
- the full test suite remains green.

prs.title: Add draft-06 contains support
prs.body:
Hi @jdesrosiers 

I made the required updates for draft-06/07 `contains` support:

Registered only the draft-06 keyword URI:
  https://json-schema.org/keyword/draft-06/contains
  (since draft-06 and draft-07 share the same keyword URI)

Added a draft-specific normalization handler at:
  src/normalization-handlers/draft-06/contains.js
  which implements the simpler draft-06 semantics (at least one matching item, no minContains/maxContains).

 Updated the shared error handler (src/error-handlers/contains.js) to treat
  `draft-06/contains` as a special case:
  - skips minContains/maxContains processing
  - always reports the default range `{ minContains: 1 }`

All tests are passing locally, and lint/type-check are clean.

Please let me know if anything else should be adjusted.

prs.title: Add support for draft-2019-09
prs.body:
@jdesrosiers 

### What’s included

 **New normalization handlers**

  * `items-draft04.js` — implements pre-2020 tuple validation semantics
  * `additionalItems-draft04.js` — implements legacy handling for items beyond the tuple
**Correct keyword registration**

* `https://json-schema.org/keyword/draft-04/items`
* `https://json-schema.org/keyword/draft-04/additionalItems`
* **New test coverage**

  * Added draft-2019-09 tests using `compatibility: "<=2019"`
  * Tests are mapped from existing `prefixItems` / `items` cases where behavior differs
 **No behavior changes** for draft-2020-12 or newer drafts

### Implementation details

* `items-draft04` mirrors the behavior of `prefixItems`:

  * Applies tuple validation by index
  * Calls `evaluateSchema` per array position
  * Always returns a `NormalizedOutput[]` (no `null` values)
* `additionalItems-draft04` mirrors the draft-2020-12 `items` handler:

  * Only applies when `items` is an array (tuple form)
  * Starts validating from `items.length`
  * Correctly handles `true`, `false`, and schema values
* Both handlers are implemented as **simple applicators**.

---

### Test status

All existing tests pass
New draft-2019-09 tests pass
Total: **200 / 200 tests passing**

---

### Notes

This implementation follows the guidance discussed in the issue:

* Explicit handlers for legacy semantics
* Test mapping approach instead of duplicating cases
* No localization changes required

Happy to adjust anything if you’d like a different structure or naming.

prs.title: perf(blog): optimize hover performance on paginated blog page
prs.body:
**What kind of change does this PR introduce?**

- Performance optimization (non-functional change)
- Minor refactor to move expensive calculations out of render

---

**Issue Number:#1965
Closes #1965
- Related to #1965

---

**Screenshots/videos:**

Before:
- Hover animations on blog cards were delayed on page 2+ of the Blog listing

After:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/78f9043c-5ffc-436a-b224-9b2fb8a271b4


(No visual UI changes)

---

**If relevant, did you update the documentation?**

- Not required (no user-facing changes)

---

**Summary**

Hover animations on the Blog page felt delayed on paginated views (page 2 and beyond).
This was caused by runtime-heavy operations being executed during render, including
reading-time calculations for each blog post.

This PR improves performance by:

- Moving reading-time calculation to build time (`getStaticProps`)  
  Reading time was previously computed during render for each blog card.
  This calculation is now performed once at build time and passed as static
  data, eliminating repeated runtime work on the client.

- Reducing main-thread work on paginated pages  
  On page 2 and beyond, multiple blog cards were rendered simultaneously,
  causing expensive computations during render and resulting in delayed
  hover interactions. By precomputing data, render work is minimized and
  hover responsiveness is improved.

- Preserving existing hover styles and UI behavior  
  No CSS, animation, or interaction logic was modified. Hover behavior
  remains exactly the same; only the underlying performance has been
  optimized.


As a result, reading time is now computed at build time to avoid repeated runtime
calculations during pagination, improving hover responsiveness without
changing UI behavior.


---

**Does this PR introduce a breaking change?**

- No. This change is internal and does not affect public APIs or UI behavior.

---

prs.title: add Java solution for Maximum Sum Such That No Two Elements Are Adjacent

labels.name: hacktoberfest-accepted
labels.description: Accepted for hacktoberfest 2025

prs.body:
Implemented a dynamic programming solution in Java to find the maximum sum of elements in an array such that no two selected elements are adjacent.

Key points:
- Handles all edge cases: empty array, single-element array, etc.
- Uses DP relation: dp[i] = max(nums[i] + dp[i-2], dp[i-1])
- Time complexity: O(n)
- Space complexity: O(n)
- Added multiple test cases for validation:
  • [3, 2, 7, 10] → 13
  • [3, 2, 5, 10, 7] → 15
  • [5] → 5
  • [5, 5, 10, 100, 10, 5] → 110
  • [] → 0

Includes detailed inline comments for clarity.

issues.body: ### Bug Details ### Description Multi-type schemas (e.g., `type: ["string", "number"]`) are incorrectly colored GRAY instead of following the documented priority order (object > array > string > number) mentioned in the README. The README states: > If multiple instance types are defined (e.g., `type: ["string", "number"]`), there is currently no dedicated color. In such cases, the node color is determined based on **type inference**, following this priority order: `object > array > string > number`. [Readme](https://github.com/ioflux-org/studio-json-schema?tab=readme-ov-file#understanding-the-visualization) However, this priority order is not being applied in the current implementation. --- ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Create a schema with multi-type properties: ```json { "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema", "type": "object", "title": "Multi-Type Color Bug Test", "properties": { "workingSingleObject": { "type": "object", "description": "✓ WORKS - Single type object shows BLUE" }, "workingSingleArray": { "type": "array", "description": "✓ WORKS - Single type array shows PURPLE" }, "workingSingleString": { "type": "string", "description": "✓ WORKS - Single type string shows YELLOW" }, "workingSingleNumber": { "type": "number", "description": "✓ WORKS - Single type number shows ORANGE" }, "brokenMultiObjectArray": { "type": ["object", "array"], "description": "✗ BUG - Should be BLUE (object priority) but shows GRAY" }, "brokenMultiArrayString": { "type": ["array", "string"], "description": "✗ BUG - Should be PURPLE (array priority) but shows GRAY" }, "brokenMultiStringNumber": { "type": ["string", "number"], "description": "✗ BUG - Should be YELLOW (string priority) but shows GRAY" }, "brokenMultiNumberNull": { "type": ["number", "null"], "description": "✗ BUG - Should be ORANGE (number priority) but shows GRAY" } } } ``` 2. Load the schema in JSON Schema Studio 3. Observe that all multi-type properties are colored GRAY instead of following the priority order ### Demo Video/Screenshots ### Expected Behavior Following the documented priority order (object > array > string > number): **Single-type schemas (already working):** - `type: "object"` → BLUE (object color) - `type: "array"` → PURPLE (array color) - `type: "string"` → YELLOW (string color) - `type: "number"` → ORANGE (number color) **Multi-type schemas (currently broken, should follow priority):** - `type: ["object", "array"]` → should show BLUE (object has highest priority) - `type: ["array", "string"]` → should show PURPLE (array has higher priority than string) - `type: ["string", "number"]` → should show YELLOW (string has higher priority than number) - `type: ["number", "null"]` → should show ORANGE (number has higher priority than null) ### Actual Behavior All multi-type schemas are colored GRAY regardless of the types in the array. ### Environment - **OS:** MAC OS - **Browser:** Chrome - **Version:** Latest ### Additional Context N/A ### Good First Issue - [ ] I believe this issue is a good first issue for new contributors. ### Contribution - [x] I am willing to fix this bug.

issues.title: Bug: "Copied" state persists across nodes — clicking a new node shows stale copy feedback

labels.name: bug
labels.description: Something isn't working

issues.body:
### Bug Details

### Description
In `src/components/NodeDetailsPopup.tsx` (lines 16–27), the `copied` state is never 
reset when `nodeId` changes. When a user clicks "Copy path" on Node A, then immediately 
clicks Node B within the 2-second timeout window, the popup for Node B incorrectly 
shows the ✓ copied indicator — even though nothing was copied from Node B.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Open the app at https://studio.ioflux.org and paste any schema with multiple nodes
2. Click on Node A to open its details popup
3. Click the "Copy path" button — the ✓ copied indicator appears
4. Immediately click on Node B within 2 seconds to open its popup
5. Observe: Node B's popup still shows ✓ copied even though nothing was copied from it

### Demo Video/Screenshots
N/A

### Expected Behavior
When switching to a different node, the copied indicator should immediately reset to 
the default copy icon. Each node's popup should reflect only its own copy state.

### Environment
- **OS:** macOS
- **Browser:** Chrome
- **Version:** 0.5.6-beta

### Additional Context
Root cause: the `copied` state is tied to the component instance, not the `nodeId`. 
When React reuses the component for a new node, the old `copied: true` state carries 
over. The `setTimeout` reset doesn't help because the component is never unmounted.

Current code in `src/components/NodeDetailsPopup.tsx`:
```ts
const [copied, setCopied] = useState(false);

const copyPathToClipboard = () => {
  if (nodeId) {
    navigator.clipboard.writeText(extractPath(nodeId));
    setCopied(true);
    setTimeout(() => setCopied(false), 2000);
  }
};
```

Fix — add a `useEffect` to reset `copied` when `nodeId` changes:
```ts
useEffect(() => {
  setCopied(false);
}, [nodeId]);
```
And update the import:
```ts
import { useState, useEffect } from "react";
```

### Contribution
I am willing to fix this and submit a PR.

### Contribution

- [ ] I am willing to fix this bug.

issues.title: ✨ Proposal: Spec does not define required behavior for invalid schemas, leading to inconsistent validator behavior

labels.name: proposal
labels.description: Initial discussion of a new idea.  A project will be created once a proposal document is created.

issues.body:
### Describe the inspiration for your proposal

Description

While reviewing the specification, I noticed that the required behavior for invalid schemas is not clearly defined.

Specifically:

The Core specification (specs/jsonschema-core.md) defines what a JSON Schema is and how keywords are evaluated, but does not normatively specify what implementations MUST do when a schema violates keyword constraints (e.g. incorrect keyword value types).

The Validation specification (specs/jsonschema-validation.md) defines assertion semantics for validation keywords, but assumes schemas are valid and does not define behavior when they are not.

As a result, the overall behavior for invalid schemas appears to be undefined.

### Describe the proposal

1. Create the following JSON Schema:

{
  "type": 123
}


2. Use this schema to validate any instance, for example:

"example"


3. Observe the behavior across different JSON Schema implementations.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

1. Leaving the behavior undefined and allowing implementations to continue handling invalid schemas in implementation-specific ways.

2. This was considered, but it continues to produce interoperability issues and makes it difficult for users and tooling to reason about schema correctness.

3. Treating invalid schemas as always-valid or always-invalid.
This simplifies implementation but can silently mask schema authoring errors or cause unexpected validation failures, both of which are undesirable for users.

4. Requiring implementations to raise errors or halt evaluation when an invalid schema is encountered.
This provides strong feedback to schema authors but may be disruptive for existing implementations that currently ignore certain errors.

5. Defining schema validation as a separate, optional pre-processing step.
While this keeps validation and evaluation concerns separated, the current specification does not clearly describe how evaluation should proceed if such validation fails.

### Additional context

labels.name: javascript
labels.description: Pull requests that update Javascript code

issues.body:
### Describe the bug

The function getFindResultsByGlobalRegExp() currently uses String.prototype.substr(), which is deprecated in modern JavaScript and Node.js runtimes. Using deprecated string APIs may lead to unexpected behavior in future JS engines and reduces maintainability.

### Steps To Reproduce

Open the file:
lib/getFindResultsByGlobalRegExp.ts

Locate the line where inMatchGroupOffset is calculated:

const inMatchGroupOffset = text.substr(groupOffset).indexOf(match);


Notice that the function uses String.prototype.substr(), which is officially deprecated in modern JavaScript and Node.js.

Search for “substr deprecated JavaScript” or check the MDN docs — you will see a deprecation notice.

Run any linter (ESLint / TypeScript strict mode).
Many setups will show a warning similar to:
“substr is deprecated and should not be used.”

Observe that using deprecated methods is considered a maintenance bug and should be replaced with slice().

### Expected Behavior

Impact:

Improves long-term compatibility

Removes deprecated API usage

Prevents potential runtime issues

Increases code maintainability

## 🎯 Problem Information
- **Problem Name**:Maximum Sum of Non-Adjacent Elements 
- **CSES Link**: N/A
- **Category**: Dynamic Programming
- **Estimated Difficulty**: [Medium]

## 📋 Problem Description

Brief description of the problem and what it asks for.
Given an array of positive integers, find the maximum sum of elements such that no two selected elements are adjacent in the array.

You can only choose elements that are not next to each other. The goal is to maximize the total sum of chosen numbers.



## 💡 Proposed Approach
Describe the algorithm/approach you plan to use:

Use a Dynamic Programming approach with space optimization:
Keep track of two values:
incl → max sum including the current element
excl → max sum excluding the current element

For each number:
Update incl as excl + nums[i]
Update excl as max(previous incl, previous excl)
The answer is max(incl, excl) after processing all elements.
Time Complexity: O(n)
Space Complexity: O(1)


## 📝 Implementation Notes
Any special considerations or optimizations needed:
- [ ] Requires fast I/O
- [ ] Needs modular arithmetic
- [ ] Large input constraints

## Summary
The "Getting Started with Ethical Hacking" learning sections would benefit from a subtle hover/focus effect to improve discoverability and perceived interactivity. Currently the list of topics (e.g. "Setup for learning", "Virtualization", "Introduction to Penetration Testing", "📌 What is RSA?" etc.) is plain text — adding a small card/hover animation will make the UI feel more modern and help users know items are clickable or selectable.

## What I propose
Add a small CSS hover + focus effect for the content items (cards or list elements). The effect should be:
- Subtle (scale + shadow + background tint)
- Respect `prefers-reduced-motion`
- Include a visible focus state for keyboard users (outline)
- Easy to drop into the current stylesheet
